task: Meta-level request about agent system self-improvement
user_message: we need our agent orchestrator to make his own agents and I need to collect data to fine tune him
good_response: Route to soul_core — this is a meta-architectural decision about the system's own evolution. soul_core handles reflection, trust, purpose, and goal arbitration. Decompose into: (1) agent factory design, (2) decision data collection, (3) training pipeline. Delegate implementation to devops_agent and data_agent after soul_core approves the plan.
bad_response: Route to devops_agent — this involves building code so devops should handle it. Start creating files immediately.
good_plan: Reflect on what self-creation means for agent governance
Define constraints: who can create agents, what tools they get, impact limits
Design the data collection strategy: what decisions to capture, what makes good vs bad
Delegate implementation to specialist agents
Validate with tests before integration
bad_plan: Just start coding the factory
Skip the governance design
Hard-code everything
why_good_is_better: soul_core must arbitrate meta-level architectural decisions before implementation begins
The good plan considers governance (who creates, impact limits) before jumping to code
DPO training data design requires understanding what 'good' means — that's soul_core's reflection domain
bad plan skips constraint analysis and would produce an ungoverned factory

task: Routing a request about collecting good vs bad decision data
user_message: I want data of good choices and bad choices for DPO fine-tuning our router
good_response: Route to soul_core — evaluating what constitutes a 'good' vs 'bad' routing decision is a reflection/trust-scoring task. soul_core defines quality criteria, then data_agent implements the collection pipeline.
bad_response: Route to data_agent — this is about data collection, so data_agent should handle it directly.
good_plan: Define what makes a routing decision 'good': correct agent, high confidence, fast latency
Define what makes it 'bad': wrong agent, boundary confusion, low confidence
Identify known weak boundaries for focused data collection
Design preference pair format matching DPO training requirements
bad_plan: Just start logging all routing calls
Don't distinguish between easy and hard decisions
Collect data without defining quality criteria
why_good_is_better: Defining quality criteria is reflection — soul_core's domain
Without soul_core defining 'good' vs 'bad', data_agent would collect undifferentiated data
DPO training specifically requires preference pairs with reasoning — that reasoning comes from soul_core's trust model
The bad approach would produce low-quality training data without boundary awareness

task: Diagnose combobox DOM structure on network hardware UI
user_message: the #policy selector can't find child .combobox-display-area, how do I set the value?
good_response: Route to it_agent — this is network hardware UI interaction. The #policy element is an INPUT.hidden.combobox-value, not a container. Navigate to parentElement to get .combobox-container, then click .combobox-switch to open dropdown, then click the target label.
bad_response: Route to code_review_agent — this is a CSS selector issue that needs code review.
good_plan: Inspect the actual DOM structure of the ER605 management UI
Identify that #policy is a hidden input, not a container element
Find the correct traversal: parentElement → combobox-container → combobox-switch → label
Test the corrected selector pattern
bad_plan: Try different CSS selectors blindly
Assume standard HTML select element behavior
Route to code_review for a non-code issue
why_good_is_better: Network hardware UI interaction is infrastructure work — it_agent territory
The good plan investigates DOM structure before assuming selectors
code_review_agent handles code diffs, not network device management UIs
Understanding proprietary combobox implementations requires infrastructure expertise

task: Fix logger API mismatch in new module
user_message: CentralLogger has no debug method, tests are failing with AttributeError
good_response: Route to code_review_agent — this is an API conformance issue. CentralLogger exposes log(), info(), error() but not debug(). Replace logger.debug() with logger.info() in the 2 affected call sites in decision_collector.py.
bad_response: Route to self_healer_agent — the tests are failing, which means something is broken and needs healing.
good_plan: Read CentralLogger class to discover available methods
Search for all logger.debug() calls in new code
Replace with the closest available method (info)
Re-run tests to confirm fix
bad_plan: Add a debug() method to CentralLogger
Catch the AttributeError and ignore it
Route to self_healer because 'tests failing' sounds like a crash
why_good_is_better: API mismatch is a code quality issue — code_review_agent's domain
self_healer handles runtime crashes, not test-time API mismatches
The good plan fixes the caller to match the API, not the API to match the caller
Adding debug() to CentralLogger would change a shared utility for one caller's convenience — bad practice

task: Decide where to persist dynamically created agent definitions
user_message: where should factory-created agents be stored?
good_response: Route to soul_core — agent identity persistence is a governance decision. Store in data/agents/factory/*.json: per-agent JSON files, isolated from static agent definitions, easy to inspect and version control. Not in SQLite (too heavyweight for agent identity), not in memory (lost on restart).
bad_response: Route to data_agent — this is about data storage, so data_agent should pick the persistence layer.
good_plan: Consider governance: agent definitions are identity, not generic data
Evaluate options: JSON files vs SQLite vs memory
Choose JSON files: inspectable, versionable, isolated namespace
Ensure factory directory is separate from static agent definitions
bad_plan: Put everything in SQLite
Store in-memory only
Mix with static agent definitions in ALL_AGENT_DEFINITIONS
why_good_is_better: Agent identity is a governance concern — soul_core decides how identity is managed
data_agent handles ETL and generic data, not agent lifecycle governance
JSON files are inspectable and git-friendly, matching the project's documentation-first principle
Mixing factory and static agents would violate isolation

task: Test assertion shows medium but actual difficulty is hard based on confidence threshold
user_message: test says difficulty should be medium for confidence 0.65 but it's actually hard
good_response: Route to code_review_agent — the test assertion doesn't match the business logic. confidence < 0.7 maps to 'hard' (not 'medium'). Fix the assertion to match the actual threshold: elif confidence >= 0.7: medium, else: hard. Also check the agent in the non-boundary test — devops_agent has a boundary with self_healer_agent.
bad_response: Route to self_healer_agent — tests are failing, something needs to be fixed and restarted.
good_plan: Read the difficulty classification logic in decision_collector.py
Verify thresholds: >=0.9 easy, >=0.7 medium, <0.7 hard
Fix assertion: 0.65 < 0.7 → difficulty should be 'hard'
Also fix boundary test: choose data_agent which has no weak boundary pair
bad_plan: Change the threshold to make 0.65 'medium'
Route to self_healer because tests are failing
Ignore the test and move on
why_good_is_better: Understanding business logic thresholds to fix test assertions is code review
The good plan reads the actual branching logic before changing assertions
self_healer handles runtime failures, not test logic corrections
Changing thresholds to match wrong tests would introduce bugs

task: Ensure new module integration doesn't cause circular imports
user_message: wire the decision collector into lex_router without breaking existing import chains
good_response: Route to devops_agent — use lazy import pattern: _get_collector() function that imports on first call, cached in module-level variable. This breaks the circular dependency chain (lex_router → decision_collector → models → agents → orchestrator → lex_router). Make recording best-effort with try/except.
bad_response: Route to code_review_agent — this involves reviewing import structure and code quality.
good_plan: Map the import chain to identify circular dependency risk
Implement lazy import pattern: _get_collector() with module-level cache
Hook _record_decision() at every exit path of resolve_agent()
Make recording non-fatal: catch exceptions, log, continue
Test that imports work in isolation
bad_plan: Add direct import at module top level
Only hook one exit path of resolve_agent
Let recording failures crash the router
why_good_is_better: Module integration and import chain management is build engineering — devops territory
code_review_agent reviews existing code but doesn't perform integration wiring
Lazy imports are a well-known pattern for circular dependency avoidance
Best-effort recording ensures the data collection never breaks routing

task: Request to scan code for leaked secrets after building new modules
user_message: scan the new agent factory code for any hardcoded secrets
good_response: Route to security_agent — secret scanning is security_agent's primary function using the secret_scanner tool (8-pattern regex). Scan backend/orchestrator/agent_factory.py, backend/ml/decision_collector.py, backend/ml/training_generator.py, backend/routes/agent_factory.py.
bad_response: Route to code_review_agent — reviewing code for issues is code review's job.
good_plan: Run secret_scanner on all 4 new files
Check for API keys, tokens, passwords, connection strings
Report findings with file:line references
bad_plan: Just grep for 'password'
Only scan one file
Route to code_review because 'checking code' sounds like review
why_good_is_better: Secret scanning is security_agent's dedicated function with purpose-built tooling
code_review checks code quality patterns, not secret exposure
security_agent's secret_scanner has 8 regex patterns optimized for this task
code_review would miss secrets that don't look like code quality issues

task: Vague message about making AI better
good_response: Route to soul_core — this is a vague meta-request about system improvement. soul_core must decompose this: What does 'smarter' mean? Better routing accuracy? Faster responses? More tools? soul_core reflects, sets goals, then delegates specific improvements to specialist agents.
bad_response: Route to knowledge_agent — search docs for how to improve AI.
good_plan: Clarify what 'smarter' means in context
Assess current performance metrics
Identify specific improvement vectors
Create actionable plan with measurable goals
bad_plan: Search docs for 'smart'
Just add more training data randomly
Route to a random specialist
why_good_is_better: Vague meta-requests require soul_core's reflection and goal decomposition
knowledge_agent would search docs but can't make architectural improvement decisions
soul_core turns vague intent into structured goals before delegating

task: Multi-step request spanning infrastructure and ML
user_message: configure the firewall, then build a training pipeline, then commit everything
good_response: Route to soul_core for task decomposition, then delegate: (1) it_agent for firewall config, (2) data_agent + devops_agent for training pipeline, (3) devops_agent for git commit. soul_core manages the handoff sequence and validates each step before proceeding.
bad_response: Route to devops_agent — it handles deployment and git, so it can do everything.
good_plan: Decompose into 3 sequential tasks
Route firewall config to it_agent
Route pipeline build to data_agent (design) + devops_agent (implementation)
Route git commit to devops_agent
Validate each step before handoff
bad_plan: Send everything to devops_agent
Try to do all 3 in parallel
Skip validation between steps
why_good_is_better: Multi-step cross-domain requests require soul_core's task decomposition
devops_agent can't configure firewalls — that's it_agent
Sequential validation prevents cascading failures
soul_core manages handoff order as the orchestrator
